requirements.title: Rust-owned agent runtime boundary
requirements.summary: Provide a native Rust coding-agent runtime for normal Spark chat, agent-turn, and codergen-adjacent execution, using crates/unified-llm-adapter as the LLM boundary and leaving Python agent modules only as oracle or compatibility assets.
acceptance_criteria: Normal Spark chat and agent-turn execution reaches Rust-owned code in crates/spark-agent-adapter without importing or dispatching through src/agent modules.
Codergen-adjacent runtime paths that need an agent turn use the Rust adapter boundary rather than Python agent provider clients.
The Rust agent runtime sends LLM requests through crates/unified-llm-adapter low-level Client.complete or Client.stream APIs.
The Rust agent runtime does not call Python unified_llm or Python src/agent provider clients for normal runtime behavior.
Existing Python agent tests may remain as oracle or compatibility tests, but distribution readiness is validated through the Rust-owned runtime path.
Runtime ownership and remaining compatibility boundaries are documented in docs/rust-rewrite-migration.md or README.md.
requirements.notes: This requirement anchors the run-specific scope: the historical Python implementation is not the delivered runtime path.

requirements.title: Session model lifecycle and configuration
requirements.summary: Implement the Session orchestrator, configuration defaults, lifecycle states, and history turn records required to run multiple sequential agent inputs.
acceptance_criteria: A Session is created with a stable UUID, ProviderProfile, ExecutionEnvironment, history, event emitter, config, state, LLM client, steering queue, follow-up queue, and subagent registry.

SessionConfig supports max_turns, max_tool_rounds_per_input, default_command_timeout_ms=10000, max_command_timeout_ms=600000, reasoning_effort, per-tool output and line limits, loop detection settings, and max_subagent_depth=1.
SessionState supports IDLE, PROCESSING, AWAITING_INPUT, and CLOSED with the transitions defined by submit, natural completion, limits, user answers, explicit close, abort, and unrecoverable errors.
Conversation history records UserTurn, AssistantTurn, ToolResultsTurn, SystemTurn, and SteeringTurn with timestamps and the fields described by the spec.
Multiple sequential inputs can be submitted to the same session after a processing cycle returns to IDLE.
Session lifecycle behavior is covered through observable events or public APIs rather than tests that inspect source text.
requirements.notes: The Rust public API may use idiomatic Rust names, but it must preserve the specified observable lifecycle semantics.

requirements.title: Core agentic loop
requirements.summary: Implement process_input as an agentic loop that alternates Rust-owned LLM calls with tool execution until natural completion, limits, abort, or unrecoverable error.
acceptance_criteria: process_input appends and emits the user input, drains pending steering before the first LLM call, and tracks per-input tool round count.
Each LLM request is built from the active provider profile's system prompt, converted session history, active tool definitions, tool_choice=auto, reasoning_effort, provider id, and provider options.
The loop uses Client.complete or Client.stream from crates/unified-llm-adapter directly and does not use a high-level SDK generate function that owns its own tool loop.
Assistant responses are recorded with text, tool calls, reasoning, usage, provider response id, and timestamp.
A response with no tool calls exits as natural completion and returns the session to IDLE after follow-up handling.
A response with tool calls executes those calls through the Rust tool execution path, appends ToolResultsTurn, drains steering injected during tool execution, and continues the loop.
Round limits, turn limits, abort signals, and unrecoverable errors stop the loop according to the specified state transitions.
The loop preserves observable behavior through typed events for user input, assistant output, tool calls, limits, warnings, errors, and processing completion.
requirements.notes: This is the central runtime behavior that replaces Python src/agent session orchestration.

requirements.title: Typed event stream
requirements.summary: Expose a typed event stream suitable for host applications, preserving assistant streaming, model-proposed tool-call events, actual tool execution events, usage updates, and session lifecycle events.
acceptance_criteria: Session events include kind, timestamp, session_id, and structured data payload.

The event stream supports SESSION_START, SESSION_END, USER_INPUT, PROCESSING_END, ASSISTANT_TEXT_START, ASSISTANT_TEXT_DELTA, ASSISTANT_TEXT_END, TOOL_CALL_START, TOOL_CALL_OUTPUT_DELTA, TOOL_CALL_END, STEERING_INJECTED, TURN_LIMIT, LOOP_DETECTION, WARNING, and ERROR.
When consuming unified LLM StreamEvent values, provider text, reasoning, model-proposed tool calls, and finish usage are converted into distinct session events.
Model-proposed tool-call events are separate from actual tool execution events.
TOOL_CALL_END carries full untruncated output even when the ToolResult sent back to the LLM is truncated.
SESSION_START and SESSION_END bracket every session, including graceful shutdown and unrecoverable error paths.
Events are delivered through an async iterator, channel, callback, or equivalent language-appropriate public interface.
Spark-facing Python adapters preserve their existing typed event contract when backed by the Rust runtime.
requirements.notes: The event stream is the host application's primary control and rendering interface.

requirements.title: Provider-aligned profiles
requirements.summary: Implement OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini ProviderProfile support with provider-native model-facing tools, prompts, options, and capability flags.
acceptance_criteria: ProviderProfile exposes id, model, tool registry, build_system_prompt, tools, provider_options, supports_reasoning, supports_streaming, supports_parallel_tool_calls, and context_window_size.

The OpenAI profile is codex-rs aligned, exposes apply_patch as the primary editing tool, includes the required core tools and subagent tools, and maps reasoning_effort to OpenAI Responses API reasoning options.

The Anthropic profile is Claude Code aligned, exposes edit_file old_string/new_string editing instead of apply_patch, applies the 120 second shell default unless timeout_ms is provided, and passes beta headers through provider options.

The Gemini profile is gemini-cli aligned, exposes Gemini-style file, edit, shell, grep, glob, list_dir, optional web tools, and subagent tools, and passes Gemini safety, grounding, and thinking options through provider options.
Each profile builds a provider-specific system prompt covering identity, tool usage, provider-native editing expectations, error handling, and coding guidance.
Additional custom tools can be registered on any profile after construction.
When a custom tool name collides with a profile tool name, the latest registration overrides the prior tool definition and executor.
Profile-facing tests validate observable tool availability and provider options, not byte-for-byte prompt strings.
requirements.notes: Provider alignment means preserving model-facing affordances, not forcing a universal tool schema.

requirements.title: Tool registry and execution pipeline
requirements.summary: Implement the Rust tool registry and dispatch pipeline that validates model-requested tools, executes registered tools through the active environment, truncates outputs, emits events, and returns ToolResult values.
acceptance_criteria: ToolDefinition includes unique name, description, and a JSON Schema parameters object.
ToolRegistry supports register, unregister, get, definitions, and names operations.
Tool dispatch performs lookup, argument parsing, JSON Schema validation, execution, truncation, event emission, and ToolResult construction in that order.
Unknown tool calls return ToolResult with is_error=true and an explanatory message instead of throwing an unrecoverable exception.
Invalid arguments return ToolResult with is_error=true and an explanatory message instead of executing the tool.
Tool execution errors are caught and returned to the LLM as error results with is_error=true.
Profiles that support parallel tool calls execute multiple calls concurrently while preserving result association by tool_call_id.
Tool execution emits TOOL_CALL_START before execution and TOOL_CALL_END after success or failure.
requirements.notes: Recoverable tool-level failures must be visible to the model so it can retry or choose another approach.

requirements.title: Built-in coding tools
requirements.summary: Implement provider-aligned built-in tools for file reads and writes, exact-match edits, OpenAI v4a apply_patch, shell execution, grep, glob, and Gemini directory or batch-read extensions where required.
acceptance_criteria: read_file reads text with line numbers, supports 1-based offset and limit, reports file-not-found, permission, and binary-file errors, and can return image data for multimodal-capable models.
write_file writes full content, creates files and parent directories as needed, and returns a bytes-written confirmation.

edit_file replaces exact old_string matches, supports replace_all=false by default, reports not-found or non-unique matches, and may report fuzzy whitespace or Unicode-equivalence matches before applying them.
The OpenAI apply_patch tool accepts v4a add, delete, update, rename, multi-hunk, and end-of-file marker operations and reports parse, file-not-found, and verification errors.
shell executes commands with stdout, stderr, exit code, timed_out, and duration_ms in the result and supports per-call timeout_ms.
grep searches regex patterns with path, glob filter, case-insensitive, and max_results options and reports invalid regex or missing path errors.
glob returns matching paths sorted by modification time newest first and reports invalid pattern or missing path errors.
Gemini profile extensions such as read_many_files and list_dir are implemented when exposed by that profile.
Provider profiles expose the editing format native to the provider and do not expose apply_patch as the Anthropic native editing path.
requirements.notes: Tool schemas may differ by profile when needed to preserve provider-native behavior.

requirements.title: Execution environment abstraction
requirements.summary: Implement the ExecutionEnvironment interface and a required local implementation for file operations, command execution, search, metadata, lifecycle cleanup, and future custom environment support.
acceptance_criteria: ExecutionEnvironment exposes read_file, write_file, file_exists, list_directory, exec_command, grep, glob, initialize, cleanup, working_directory, platform, and os_version operations.
LocalExecutionEnvironment resolves paths relative to working_directory for local filesystem operations.
Local command execution starts commands in a new process group and uses the platform shell, including /bin/bash -c on Linux and macOS and cmd.exe /c on Windows.
Command execution enforces a default 10000ms timeout and a configurable 600000ms maximum timeout unless a provider-specific profile default applies.
On timeout, the local environment sends SIGTERM to the process group, waits 2 seconds, sends SIGKILL to remaining processes, and returns collected output plus the specified timeout message.
Environment filtering excludes variables matching *_API_KEY, *_SECRET, *_TOKEN, *_PASSWORD, and *_CREDENTIAL case-insensitively by default.
Environment policy can inherit all variables, inherit none, or inherit only core safe variables such as PATH, HOME, USER, SHELL, LANG, TERM, TMPDIR, GOPATH, CARGO_HOME, and NVM_DIR.
grep uses ripgrep when available and falls back to a native regex search when ripgrep is unavailable.
The public abstraction can be implemented by consumers for Docker, Kubernetes, WASM, SSH, logging wrappers, or read-only wrappers without changing tool logic.
requirements.notes: The default implementation is local, but the architecture must keep tool logic independent from execution location.

requirements.title: Tool output truncation
requirements.summary: Apply deterministic output truncation before ToolResult values are sent to the LLM while preserving full tool output in events for host applications.
acceptance_criteria: Character-based truncation runs first for every tool output before any line-based truncation.
head_tail truncation keeps the beginning and end of output and inserts a warning that states how many characters were removed from the middle and that full output is available in the event stream.
tail truncation keeps the end of output and inserts a warning that states how many leading characters were removed and that full output is available in the event stream.

Default character limits and modes match the spec: read_file 50000 head_tail, shell 30000 head_tail, grep 20000 tail, glob 20000 tail, edit_file 10000 tail, apply_patch 10000 tail, write_file 1000 tail, spawn_agent 20000 head_tail.
Line-based truncation runs second where configured with defaults shell=256, grep=200, glob=500, read_file=None, edit_file=None.
Line truncation uses a head/tail split with a visible omitted-lines marker.
SessionConfig can override per-tool character and line limits.
A pathological output with very few extremely long lines is still bounded by character truncation.
TOOL_CALL_END events expose full untruncated output while ToolResult content contains the truncated output.
requirements.notes: The model must be told when it is seeing incomplete output.

requirements.title: System prompts environment context and project docs
requirements.summary: Build provider-specific system prompts from layered instructions, structured environment context, git context, tool descriptions, project documentation, and user overrides.
acceptance_criteria: System prompt construction layers provider-specific base instructions, environment context, active tool descriptions, project-specific instructions, and user instruction overrides in that order.
Provider-specific base instructions cover the topics required for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini profiles without requiring tests to assert entire prompt strings.
Environment context includes working directory, git repository status, git branch, platform, OS version, today's date, model display name, and knowledge cutoff when known.
Git context is snapshotted at session start and includes current branch, short modified and untracked counts, and recent commit messages.
Project document discovery walks from git root or working directory to the active working directory and loads root-level files before deeper files.
AGENTS.md is always loaded when present; provider-specific files are loaded only for the active profile: CLAUDE.md for Anthropic, GEMINI.md for Gemini, and .codex/instructions.md for OpenAI.
Project instruction loading enforces a 32KB total budget and appends the marker [Project instructions truncated at 32KB] when truncation occurs.
Steering and project documentation are converted to user-role or system-prompt content according to the spec so provider request translation receives the correct roles.
Approximate context usage is tracked with the 1 token ~= 4 characters heuristic and emits a WARNING event above 80 percent of the provider context window without automatic compaction.
requirements.notes: AGENTS.md instructions in this repository remain input to the runtime, not implementation source text for tests.

requirements.title: Steering follow-ups and runtime reasoning controls
requirements.summary: Support host-controlled steering, queued follow-up inputs, and dynamic reasoning_effort changes between model calls.
acceptance_criteria: session.steer queues a message for injection after the current tool round completes.
If the session is idle, a steering message is retained and injected on the next submit before the first LLM call.
Injected steering messages become SteeringTurn entries in history and emit STEERING_INJECTED.
SteeringTurn entries are converted to user-role messages for the next LLM request.
session.follow_up queues a message that starts a new processing cycle after the current input naturally completes.
Queued follow-up processing preserves the same session history and event stream semantics as a normal submit.
reasoning_effort accepts low, medium, high, null, and provider-supported extensions without breaking provider-default behavior.
Changing reasoning_effort mid-session takes effect on the next LLM request.
Provider profiles map reasoning_effort to the unified Request and provider-specific options required by OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini.
requirements.notes: Steering is a programmable library feature and must not require restarting the session.

requirements.title: Stop conditions and loop detection
requirements.summary: Enforce natural completion, round limits, session turn limits, abort cancellation, unrecoverable error handling, context warnings, and repeated-tool loop detection.
acceptance_criteria: Natural completion exits the loop when the model response contains no tool calls.
max_tool_rounds_per_input emits TURN_LIMIT and stops the current input after the configured number of tool rounds.
max_turns emits TURN_LIMIT and stops processing when total session turns reach the configured limit.
Abort signals stop the loop, initiate graceful shutdown, and transition the session to CLOSED.
Unrecoverable errors transition the session to CLOSED after graceful cleanup.
Context window overflow is reported as a WARNING event and does not trigger automatic compaction or summarization.
Loop detection tracks tool call signatures as name plus arguments hash.
When the last configured window of tool calls repeats a pattern of length 1, 2, or 3, the runtime appends a SteeringTurn warning and emits LOOP_DETECTION.
Loop detection is enabled by default with a window of 10 and can be disabled through SessionConfig.
requirements.notes: Loop detection should steer recovery without silently changing model output.

requirements.title: Subagent tools and lifecycle
requirements.summary: Allow a parent agent to spawn, communicate with, wait for, and close scoped child sessions that share the execution environment while maintaining independent histories.
acceptance_criteria: spawn_agent accepts task, optional working_dir, optional model override, and optional max_turns and returns an agent id with initial status.
send_input sends a message to a running subagent and returns an acknowledgement.
wait waits for subagent completion and returns output text, success boolean, and turns_used.
close_agent terminates a subagent and returns final status.
Each subagent has an independent Session and conversation history.
Subagents share the parent's ExecutionEnvironment and therefore operate on the same filesystem and working directory scope unless a subdirectory is supplied.
Subagents use the parent's ProviderProfile unless an allowed model override is supplied.
Depth limiting prevents recursive sub-sub-agent spawning by default with max_subagent_depth=1.
Subagent tool results are returned to the parent as ToolResult values and surfaced in the parent's event stream.
requirements.notes: M5 implemented Rust subagent tool registration and lifecycle behavior through provider registries, child sessions, shared environment scoping, model override policy, depth limits, parent-visible results, recoverable failures, and shutdown cleanup.

requirements.title: Error handling and graceful shutdown
requirements.summary: Handle recoverable tool and provider failures without collapsing the session, surface unrecoverable failures correctly, and cleanly shut down LLM streams, processes, events, and subagents.
acceptance_criteria: Tool-level errors such as file not found, edit conflicts, shell nonzero exit, shell timeout, permission denied, validation error, and unknown tool are returned to the LLM as is_error=true ToolResult values.
LLM API transient errors such as HTTP 429, HTTP 500-503, and network errors are retried with backoff by the unified LLM adapter layer.
Authentication errors surface immediately without retry and transition the session to CLOSED.
Context length errors emit WARNING and allow the session to continue when possible.
TurnLimitExceeded emits TURN_LIMIT and returns the session to IDLE rather than CLOSED.

Abort or unrecoverable error shutdown cancels in-flight LLM streams, terminates all running command process groups, waits 2 seconds, kills remaining processes, flushes pending events, closes active subagents, emits SESSION_END, and transitions to CLOSED.
The runtime records enough structured error detail for Spark adapters to preserve existing thread resume and failure reporting behavior.
requirements.notes: M4 completed recoverable tool errors, provider error taxonomy, context warnings, turn-limit behavior, command and stream abort cleanup, event flushing, and Spark-compatible structured error payloads. M5 completed subagent-specific shutdown cleanup for active child handles.

requirements.title: Spark chat and codergen integration
requirements.summary: Wire the Rust agent runtime into Spark-facing chat session and codergen backend paths while preserving the existing observable API, event, token, raw-log, and failure contracts.
acceptance_criteria: src/spark/chat/session.py routes normal agent-backed chat turns to the Rust agent adapter or a Rust-backed process boundary instead of Python src/agent modules.
src/attractor/api/codex_backends.py routes normal agent-turn or codergen-adjacent execution to the Rust agent adapter where the existing contract expects agent behavior.

Spark-facing APIs continue to expose the same user-visible session lifecycle, typed event stream, final assistant text, token usage, raw log lines, thread resume failures, and error reporting shapes expected by existing callers.
Provider, model, llm_profile, reasoning_effort, project_path, conversation_id, chat_mode, and metadata from Spark request objects are preserved across the Rust boundary.
The integration can run without importing src/agent during normal runtime startup or turn execution.
Compatibility tests cover the Python facade behavior using observable outputs from real interfaces rather than checking repository text.
Any retained Python code under src/agent is clearly limited to oracle, compatibility, fallback, or historical implementation status and is not required for the normal distributed runtime.
requirements.notes: M6A1 completed the Workspace service-level Rust agent turn execution sub-scope. M6A2 completed HTTP route wiring through the Workspace service-level Rust AgentTurnBackend path, including backend-ingested responses and live envelope publication. M6A3 completed request-user-input answer handling through the Rust backend lifecycle for Workspace and HTTP, including backend continuation, public answer semantics, and live publication. M6A4 completed Workspace/HTTP output-contract closure for persisted snapshots, live envelopes, route responses, usage, raw logs, request-user-input records, thread resume failures, and structured errors. M6B completed Python Spark chat and codergen facade routing through fakeable or committed Rust boundary calls with selector normalization and Python agent import isolation. M6C completed Rust Attractor codergen-adjacent runtime integration through spark-agent-adapter, including text-only low-level requests, Session-backed agent execution, codergen contract artifacts, usage and failure payloads, and child intervention delivery. M6 closure validated the combined integration boundary, documentation truthfulness, public compatibility coverage, and repository-wide uv run pytest -q gate.

requirements.title: Behavioral validation and cross-provider parity
requirements.summary: Validate the Rust-owned runtime with observable behavior tests, compatibility tests, cross-provider parity coverage, and optional live smoke tests.
acceptance_criteria: Unit and integration tests cover all Definition of Done areas: core loop, provider profiles, tool execution, execution environment, truncation, steering, reasoning effort, prompts, subagents, events, and error handling.

Cross-provider parity tests cover OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini for simple file creation, read-then-edit, multi-file edits, shell execution, shell timeout, grep plus glob, multi-step tasks, large-output truncation, parallel calls when supported, steering, reasoning effort change, subagent spawn and wait, loop detection, error recovery, and provider-specific editing format.
Tests assert observable behavior through public APIs, CLI or API responses, event streams, filesystem effects, state transitions, and tool results.
Tests do not depend on provider prompt source strings, deprecated implementation details, or harmless source-code wording.
Live smoke tests with real API keys are available for the integration sequence in Section 9.13 and are safely skipped when credentials are absent.
The repository's required validation command uv run pytest -q passes before a code-change milestone is reported complete.
Failure triage uses uv run pytest -q -x --maxfail=1 with an appropriate path or node id to find the first actionable error.
requirements.notes: The AGENTS.md test policy applies to all implementation milestones in this run.

requirements.title: Distribution readiness documentation
requirements.summary: Document how the Rust-native coding-agent runtime is configured, integrated, validated, and distinguished from out-of-scope extensions and historical Python implementation status.
acceptance_criteria: docs/rust-rewrite-migration.md describes the Rust agent runtime boundary, Spark chat and codergen integration points, and the remaining role of Python src/agent modules.
README.md or migration documentation explains required provider configuration, profile selection, reasoning_effort, project instruction loading, and validation commands for the Rust runtime.

Documentation identifies out-of-scope extensions from the spec such as MCP, skills, sandbox policies, automatic compaction, approval systems, and read-before-write guardrails unless separately implemented.
Documentation describes how to run compatibility tests, Rust adapter tests exposed through pytest, and optional live smoke tests.
Documentation gives host applications enough information to consume the event stream, steer sessions, supply follow-ups, and swap execution environments.
Docs avoid claiming the historical Python agent ledger completes this run unless the normal runtime path is Rust-owned and validated.
requirements.notes: This requirement keeps distribution guidance aligned with the Rust rewrite scope rather than historical Python implementation status.
